messages.say:
Thought for 52s


This file is a standalone Python 3 script called “Enhanced File Scanner v2.0”. It walks a directory tree, collects registry-ready metadata for every directory + file, and writes results out streaming-style to one or more output formats (JSON / NDJSON / CSV / Markdown), with resume/checkpoint support and structured error capture.


Enhanced File Scanner v2


What it’s for


Inventory + metadata dump of a filesystem subtree (intended to be “registry-ready”).
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Supports interrupted scans (CTRL+C / SIGTERM) with checkpoint resume.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Supports an identity/naming policy for repositories that use a 16-digit ID prefix in filenames (e.g., 1234567890123456_filename.ext) and marks files that “need an ID”.
Enhanced File Scanner v2


Key configuration (top “USER CONFIGURATION SECTION”)


By default it’s set to scan a Windows path and output into a scan_output folder under it, and it outputs Markdown + CSV unless changed.


Enhanced File Scanner v2


Other notable toggles:


Hashing: INCLUDE_HASHES plus algorithms/size caps; content_hash SHA-256 is treated as the primary natural-key hash.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Filtering: exclude directory names like .git, node_modules, venv, etc.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Resume/checkpoint: enabled by default.
Enhanced File Scanner v2


What metadata it captures (record schema)


Each scanned item is represented as a FileMetadata dataclass containing:


Scan invariants: scan_id, scan_root, first_seen_utc
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Core fields: relative_path (portable), absolute path, name, extension, size_bytes, timestamps, directory/symlink flags, permissions/owner/group, mime_type
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Identity fields: extracted doc_id (16-digit prefix), has_id_prefix, needs_id
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Planning placeholders: type_code, ns_code, scope, plus empty planned_id / planned_rel_path columns (intended to be filled later by a planning step).
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Hashing: content_hash (primary SHA-256) + optional hashes map if extra algorithms are enabled.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Error capture: error_kind + error even when a row is emitted on failures.
Enhanced File Scanner v2


Identity / naming logic (16-digit prefix system)


Uses a configurable regex (default ^\d{16}_) to detect an ID prefix.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Extracts the 16-digit doc_id from the filename, sets has_id_prefix, and flags needs_id = not has_id_prefix.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Derives:
type_code from extension (or DIR / NOEXT)
Enhanced File Scanner v2

ns_code from the top-level directory (or ROOT)
Enhanced File Scanner v2

scope from ns_code mapping or a default scope
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Can override identity rules via an external JSON config (--identity-config).
Enhanced File Scanner v2


Hashing behavior (important detail)


Even if INCLUDE_HASHES = False, it still attempts to compute the primary content_hash = SHA-256 (unless size limits block it or the item is a symlink).


Enhanced File Scanner v2


Optional extra hash columns (md5/sha1/sha256/sha512) are only included when include_hashes is enabled and size constraints allow.


Enhanced File Scanner v2


Outputs it generates


It can write any combination of:


JSON array (.json, optionally .gz)

NDJSON (.ndjson, optionally .gz)

CSV (registry-oriented columns)

Markdown report (a streamed inventory table)


CSV columns include identity/planning fields and a special column that shows what the filename would look like with a placeholder prefix (0000000000000000_filename).


Enhanced File Scanner v2


Markdown includes an “Inventory” table for files only (directories are intentionally skipped in the Markdown table).


Enhanced File Scanner v2


Resume/checkpoint model


Maintains a checkpoint.txt of processed paths and will skip them on restart.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Saves checkpoints periodically (default every 1000 paths).
Enhanced File Scanner v2

On resume, if a directory root is in the checkpoint set, it prunes the whole subtree.
Enhanced File Scanner v2


Traversal + filtering behavior


Uses os.walk() over the scan root.
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Skips the output directory if it’s inside the scan path (prevents scanning its own outputs).
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Supports excluded directory names (default includes .git, node_modules, venv folders).
Enhanced File Scanner v2


How you run it


Run directly (uses the constants in the “USER CONFIGURATION SECTION”), or override via CLI args like scan_path, --output-dir, --formats, --include-hashes, --identity-config, etc.
Enhanced File Scanner v2


Notable gaps / gotchas


parallel_hashing exists in config but hashing is performed synchronously (no actual parallel worker use is present in the shown code).
Enhanced File Scanner v2

include_extended_attrs exists but there’s no implementation populating extended_attrs in the scan path shown (it remains a placeholder field).
Enhanced File Scanner v2

Thought for 21s


Add these to your “Directory Health Report” toolbox. They’re all extractable from a directory tree (some metadata-only, some require light/deep file inspection).



8. Filesystem Hygiene & Risk (Windows/Linux portability)


Signals that a directory will break automation, Git, packaging, or cross-platform use.


Path length risk
Count of paths > 240 chars (Windows pain threshold), max path length, top offenders.

Invalid / problematic names
Windows reserved names (CON, PRN, etc.), trailing dots/spaces, control chars, non-printables.

Case-collision risk
Files that differ only by case (Readme.md vs README.md) → breaks on case-insensitive FS.

Non-ASCII / normalization issues
Unicode normalization conflicts (NFC/NFD), “look-alike” characters.

Illegal/suspicious characters frequency
: * ? etc. (platform-dependent), plus counts by character.



9. Permissions, Ownership & Attributes (Security posture)


This is one of the best “state” lenses because it tells you who can mutate what.


Ownership distribution
Top owners/groups, orphaned ownership, unexpected owner hotspots.

ACL/permissions anomalies
World-writable / broadly writable areas, broken inheritance, read-only clusters.

Executable surface area
Count of executables/scripts (.exe, .dll, .ps1, .bat, .sh, .py) and where they live.

Hidden/system attributes
Hidden/system/read-only counts and locations (Windows attributes).

Recent permission changes
If you snapshot, track ACL drift over time.



10. Repository & Automation Signals (VCS/build/CI health)


This tells you if the directory behaves like a maintainable project.


VCS presence & hygiene
.git present, nested repos count, submodule presence, .gitignore coverage (what % would be ignored).

Dirty working tree indicators (optional if you call Git)
Untracked count, modified count, ignored-but-present size.

Build artifact detection
Size and count in dist/, build/, target/, bin/, obj/, .pytest_cache/, .mypy_cache/, __pycache__/.

“Artifact-to-source ratio” (artifact bytes / source bytes).

CI/CD configuration coverage
Presence of workflows (.github/workflows), pipeline configs, missing expected files.



11. Dependency & Environment Footprint


Helps you understand “what this directory needs to run.”


Manifest inventory
requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, pom.xml, go.mod, etc.

Lockfile completeness
Manifest present but no lockfile (risk), multiple lockfiles (conflict risk).

Virtual env / vendor detection
venv/, .venv/, node_modules/, vendored libs counts/sizes.



12. Content Sensitivity & Secret Risk (light content scan)


Do this as a separate “content pass” with strict limits (first N KB, known filenames).


Credential-bearing file discovery
.env, *.pem, id_rsa, *.p12, *.kdbx, cloud creds patterns.

High-risk strings (heuristics)
“API_KEY=”, “BEGIN PRIVATE KEY”, AWS formats, OAuth tokens patterns.

PII indicators (if relevant)
Emails, SSNs, phone numbers counts (as a risk metric, not full extraction).

Encryption/archive hotspots
Count/size of .zip, .7z, .rar, .gpg, .enc (can hide risk + duplication).



13. Linkage & Reference Integrity (graph health)


Measures how well files refer to each other.


Broken symlinks / junctions (you already have symlinks—extend to junctions on Windows)

Broken internal references (optional)
Markdown links to missing files, config references to missing paths.

Orphaned files
Files not referenced by any manifest/index/registry (heuristic).



14. Project Governance / SSOT-Specific Metrics (your system)


These give you “compliance state,” not just folder state.


ID coverage by subtree
% of files with SSOT prefix per folder; “worst folders” list.

ID uniqueness and collision
Duplicate IDs across different paths, multiple IDs in one filename, malformed IDs.

Registry alignment
Do filenames/paths match what a registry expects (type_code/ns_code/scope patterns).

Expected skeleton presence
Required directories/files present/missing (your “required artifacts” contract).

Deterministic snapshot signature
A stable “inventory hash” over sorted (relative_path, size, mtime, content_hash?) to detect drift.



15. Change Dynamics (churn + stability)


More informative than just “last modified spread.”


Hotspots
Folders with most modifications in last 7/30/90 days.

Churn rate
% of files changed recently and “top 20 files by change frequency” (requires snapshots).

Volatility index
Standard deviation of mtimes per folder (folders that churn vs stable archives).



16. Quality-of-Life Metrics (human navigability)


These predict operator pain.


“Too flat” and “too deep” flags
Folders with >N files; folders with single-child chains (path tunneling).

Search friction
Duplicate basenames, similar names, lots of “final_v2_reallyfinal” patterns (regex heuristic).

Documentation coverage
Presence of README per top-level folder; ratio of docs to code.



Practical way to structure this in your suite


Implement “passes” so the suite can scale:


Pass A (metadata-only): fast, safe, always-on (most of sections 1–6 + 8–10 basics).

Pass B (light content): bounded reads (first N KB, only whitelisted file types) for sections 5/7/12/13.

Pass C (deep content): full hashes, full scans, secret detection, reference graph (slow; optional).


If you want, I can turn this into a strict JSON schema for DirectoryHealthReport with: summary, rollups_by_dir, anomalies[], compliance[], and a snapshot_signature block so results are comparable across runs.
